stem: Let \( f \) be differentiable on all of \( \mathbb{R} \), with \( f'(x) = [[a]](x-[[r1]]) \) for every \( x \). How many relative extrema does \( f \) have?
solution: \( f' \) is linear with a single simple real zero at \( x=[[r1]] \), and it changes sign there, so \( f \) has exactly one relative extremum.
distractor_rationales: This assumes a critical point never produces an extremum, but a simple zero of \( f' \) always changes sign.
This overcounts, as if \( f' \) had two zeros instead of one.
This overcounts, as if \( f' \) had three zeros instead of one.
This overcounts, as if \( f' \) had four zeros instead of one.

stem: Let \( f \) be differentiable on all of \( \mathbb{R} \), with \( f'(x) = [[a]](x-[[r1]])(x-[[r2]]) \) for every \( x \). How many relative extrema does \( f \) have?
solution: \( f' \) is quadratic with two distinct simple real zeros, \( x=[[r1]] \) and \( x=[[r2]] \), and it changes sign at each, so \( f \) has exactly two relative extrema.
distractor_rationales: This counts only one of the two sign changes of \( f' \).
This overcounts, as if \( f' \) had a third zero.
This confuses the count with the degree of \( f \) (which is 3), not the number of extrema.
This assumes none of the critical points are genuine extrema, but both simple zeros of \( f' \) change sign.

stem: Let \( f \) be differentiable on all of \( \mathbb{R} \), with \( f'(x) = [[a]](x-[[r1]])(x-[[r2]])(x-[[r3]]) \) for every \( x \). How many relative extrema does \( f \) have?
solution: \( f' \) is cubic with three distinct simple real zeros, \( x=[[r1]], [[r2]], [[r3]] \), and it changes sign at each one, so \( f \) has exactly three relative extrema.
distractor_rationales: This misses one of the three sign changes of \( f' \).
This confuses the count with the degree of \( f \) (which is 4), not the number of extrema.
This wrongly assumes only the middle zero produces a genuine extremum.
This overcounts, as if \( f' \) had a fourth zero.

stem: Let \( f \) be differentiable on all of \( \mathbb{R} \), with \( f'(x) = [[a]](x-[[r1]])(x-[[r2]])(x-[[r3]])(x-[[r4]]) \) for every \( x \). How many relative extrema does \( f \) have?
solution: \( f' \) has four distinct simple real zeros, \( x=[[r1]], [[r2]], [[r3]], [[r4]] \), and it changes sign at each one, so \( f \) has exactly four relative extrema.
distractor_rationales: This misses one of the four sign changes of \( f' \).
This confuses the count with the degree of \( f \) (which is 5), not the number of extrema.
This drastically undercounts, keeping only half of the sign changes.
This overcounts, as if \( f' \) had a fifth zero.

stem: Let \( f \) be differentiable on all of \( \mathbb{R} \), with \( f'(x) = [[a]](x-[[r1]])(x-[[r2]])^{2}(x-[[r3]]) \) for every \( x \). How many CRITICAL POINTS does \( f \) have (count each distinct real number where \( f'(x)=0 \) once)?
solution: \( f'(x)=0 \) exactly when \( x=[[r1]] \), \( x=[[r2]] \), or \( x=[[r3]] \) — three distinct real numbers — regardless of the repeated factor at \( x=[[r2]] \), so \( f \) has three critical points.
distractor_rationales: This misses \( x=[[r2]] \), perhaps because it is a repeated (double) root, but it is still a critical point.
This double-counts the repeated root \( x=[[r2]] \) as if it were two distinct critical points.
This keeps only one critical point instead of all three distinct real zeros of \( f' \).
This overcounts, as if \( f' \) had a fourth distinct real zero.

stem: Let \( f \) be differentiable on all of \( \mathbb{R} \), with \( f'(x) = [[a]](x-[[r1]])(x-[[r2]])^{2}(x-[[r3]]) \) for every \( x \). How many RELATIVE EXTREMA does \( f \) have?
solution: \( f' \) is zero at \( x=[[r1]], [[r2]], [[r3]] \), but at the DOUBLE root \( x=[[r2]] \) the factor \( (x-[[r2]])^{2}\ge 0 \) never changes sign, so \( f' \) does not change sign there and \( x=[[r2]] \) is NOT an extremum. Only the simple roots \( x=[[r1]] \) and \( x=[[r3]] \) are relative extrema, so \( f \) has exactly two.
distractor_rationales: This wrongly counts the double root \( x=[[r2]] \) as an extremum too, even though \( f' \) does not change sign there.
This confuses the count with the degree of \( f \) (which is 4).
This keeps only one of the two genuine sign-changing extrema.
This assumes none of the critical points are genuine extrema.

stem: Let \( f \) be differentiable on the open interval \( 0 < x < 2\pi \), with \( f'(x) = [[a]]\sin([[n]]x) \). How many relative extrema does \( f \) have on this interval?
solution: On \( (0,2\pi) \), \( \sin([[n]]x)=0 \) exactly at \( x=\frac{m\pi}{[[n]]} \) for \( m=1,2,\dots,2\cdot[[n]]-1 \), giving \( 2([[n]])-1=[[answer]] \) simple (sign-changing) zeros, each a relative extremum of \( f \).
distractor_rationales: This overcounts by including an endpoint of the open interval, where \( f' \) is not actually zero.
This severely undercounts, counting only \( [[n]] \) of the \( 2\cdot[[n]]-1 \) sign changes.
This overcounts by including both endpoints as if the interval were closed.
This is off by two from the correct count.
